refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined design of the NGHE solution, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a mechanism (sensor integration, data logging, reporting interface) to capture energy_used and ton_moved over the evaluation period, and that reported values can be independently validated against ground truth measurements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (tonnage moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate and report the energy efficiency metric (energy_per_ton) at defined intervals.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is atomic in intent but decomposes into three atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a single functional obligation without prescribing implementation (sensor type, communication protocol, or computational location).

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.

DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into a unified telemetry system that streams both signals to a central aggregator; compute energy_per_ton on-device or in-cloud with synchronized timestamps.

DP3: Decompose measurement into two independent subsystems: (A) energy accounting via fuel consumption or power draw monitoring, (B) work quantification via load cells or displacement sensors; integrate results through a data fusion layer that normalizes units and time windows.

DP4: Implement a hierarchical measurement architecture: primary sensors (energy, work) feed into a local edge controller that performs real-time validation and buffering; edge controller transmits validated records to a reporting backend that computes and archives energy_per_ton metrics.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose measurement into two independent subsystems: (A) energy accounting via fuel consumption or power draw monitoring, (B) work quantification via load cells or displacement sensors; integrate results through a data fusion layer that normalizes units and time windows.

potential_sources: Temporal synchronization: energy and work measurements must be time-aligned; mitigate by enforcing synchronized clocks or relative timestamp correlation in the fusion layer.
Unit normalization: energy (kWh, MJ) and work (tons, kg) operate in different domains; mitigate by defining explicit conversion rules and validation thresholds in the fusion layer.
Sensor failure modes: independent failure of energy or work sensor creates incomplete records; mitigate by defining fallback logic and data quality flags.
Calibration drift: energy and work sensors may drift independently; mitigate by scheduling independent calibration cycles and versioning calibration coefficients.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves low coupling by isolating energy and work measurement concerns into separate subsystems with a thin, well-defined integration layer. This supports independent evolution, testing, and replacement of either subsystem without redesign of the other. The data fusion layer is the critical integration point and must be designed with explicit contracts (schema, timing, error handling).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is measurable via continuous monitoring (fuel flow, power draw, or equivalent proxy). RISK: If energy source is intermittent or indirect (e.g., grid-supplied with unknown baseline), measurement accuracy may degrade. MITIGATION: Validate energy measurement method against acceptance criteria during pilot.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) is directly observable and quantifiable. RISK: If work definition is ambiguous (e.g., partial loads, idle cycles), tonnage attribution may be disputed. MITIGATION: Establish clear operational definition of 'ton_moved' in worksite safety plan and validate with stakeholders.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and sensor noise. RISK: Short evaluation windows may amplify measurement uncertainty. MITIGATION: Define minimum evaluation period (e.g., ≥1 week) in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Data fusion layer can reliably synchronize and correlate energy and work records. RISK: Clock drift, packet loss, or buffering delays may introduce systematic bias. MITIGATION: Implement redundant timestamping (GPS, NTP) and validation checksums.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite operational policies permit continuous sensor deployment and data logging. RISK: Privacy, security, or operational constraints may limit sensor placement or data retention. MITIGATION: Align sensor architecture with worksite safety plan early in design.

ASSUMPTION: Energy_per_ton metric is stable and repeatable across evaluation period. RISK: If operational conditions vary significantly (e.g., terrain, load distribution, operator skill), metric may not be representative. MITIGATION: Stratify results by operational mode and document variance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Measurement granularity (e.g., per-shift, per-load, continuous); (2) Acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% on energy_per_ton); (3) Data retention and reporting frequency (e.g., daily summary, real-time dashboard); (4) Operational boundary conditions (e.g., ambient temperature range, load type constraints). This will enable DP selection to be validated against quantitative acceptance criteria and reduce ambiguity in the data fusion layer design.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement decomposition and data fusion layer that orchestrates energy accounting (fuel/power monitoring) and work quantification (load cells/displacement sensors) subsystems, normalizes heterogeneous units and time windows, and exposes unified energy-work metrics to external consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports normalized energy and work measurements, fusion results, and diagnostic data on measurement subsystem health to Vehicle and Teleoperation for monitoring and logging.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task context (mission parameters, load profiles, expected work ranges) to configure measurement windows and fusion thresholds dynamically.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals anomalies detected during fusion (e.g., energy-work mismatch exceeding tolerance, sensor failure) to Safety Services for intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits fused measurement results to remote teleoperation endpoints and receives calibration/configuration updates from remote operators.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is the natural home for measurement fusion logic, energy accounting, and work quantification. Both subsystems (A and B) and the data fusion layer are core to NGHE's mission of energy-work characterization. Minor diffusion: sensor hardware drivers may reside in Vehicle; telemetry export and safety alerts require external interface contracts but logic remains internal.

Poor containment: Vehicle is primarily a hardware abstraction and control layer. While it may host sensor drivers and low-level acquisition, the decomposition logic, unit normalization, and fusion algorithm are domain-specific to NGHE and would be scattered across Vehicle and NGHE. High diffusion of fusion logic across module boundaries.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a remote interface and command layer, not a measurement processing layer. Placing decomposition and fusion here violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling between remote operations and core measurement logic. Fusion algorithm would diffuse across NGHE, Vehicle, and Teleoperation.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is a reactive monitoring and intervention layer. It should consume fused measurements and alert on anomalies, not own the decomposition and fusion logic itself. Placing DP here inverts the dependency and scatters measurement logic across Safety Services and NGHE.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (measurement decomposition and data fusion) is a core algorithmic and data-processing responsibility that belongs in NGHE Solution. NGHE is the domain expert for energy-work characterization; it owns the fusion logic, normalization rules, and time-window alignment. Vehicle provides sensor drivers and hardware abstraction; Teleoperation and Safety Services consume the fused results via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert). This assignment maximizes cohesion within NGHE and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Sensor driver dependencies: If sensor acquisition logic is tightly coupled to Vehicle hardware abstraction, NGHE must define clear contracts (e.g., standardized sensor data packets) to avoid creeping dependencies on Vehicle internals.

Real-time fusion latency: If fusion algorithm runs synchronously with high-frequency sensor streams, latency may impact Vehicle control loops; recommend asynchronous buffering and configurable fusion windows to decouple timing.

Unit normalization complexity: If measurement units or time-window semantics are not standardized across fuel, power, load, and displacement sensors, fusion logic may become brittle; recommend a canonical measurement schema and validation layer.

Calibration and configuration drift: If sensor calibration or fusion thresholds are updated remotely via Network Link, NGHE must ensure atomic updates and version consistency to avoid transient measurement errors.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by decomposing energy consumption measurement into two independent, verifiable subsystems (energy accounting and work quantification) and integrating them through a data fusion layer. The DP is concrete, testable, and maps clearly to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved). The two-subsystem decomposition with normalization is a sound logical solution to the measurement problem.

diffusion_risks: Sensor driver coupling: NGHE Solution depends on Vehicle-hosted sensor drivers (fuel consumption, power draw, load cells, displacement sensors). If driver interfaces are not standardized and versioned, NGHE fusion logic may become brittle to Vehicle hardware changes. Recommend explicit sensor data packet schema and contract versioning.

Real-time latency creep: If fusion algorithm synchronously processes high-frequency sensor streams, latency may propagate into Vehicle control loops or telemetry export. Recommend asynchronous buffering, configurable fusion windows, and documented latency SLAs to prevent implicit coupling.

Unit normalization brittleness: Heterogeneous sensor units (liters/gallons for fuel, watts/amps for power, newtons for load, meters for displacement) require canonical normalization. If normalization rules are scattered across sensor adapters or embedded in fusion logic, maintenance and validation become fragile. Recommend centralized measurement schema with validation layer.

Calibration update atomicity: Remote calibration and fusion threshold updates via Network Link must be atomic and versioned. If updates are applied incrementally or without version tracking, transient measurement errors and inconsistent energy_per_ton calculations may occur. Recommend transactional update protocol.

Time-window alignment: Energy accounting and work quantification may operate on different sampling rates and time windows. If alignment logic is implicit or ad-hoc, fusion results may be misaligned or lose fidelity. Recommend explicit time-window negotiation and buffering strategy.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the correct ownership boundary. The decomposition logic, fusion algorithm, unit normalization, and time-window alignment are domain-specific to energy-work characterization and belong in NGHE. Vehicle provides sensor drivers and hardware abstraction (appropriate 0.45 containment); Teleoperation and Safety Services consume fused results via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking). Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and localized to sensor driver contracts and external interface dependencies. No alternative module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is suitable for DP ownership; all show poor containment (0.15–0.55) and high diffusion (0.55–0.85).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution's fused outputs. Energy accounting subsystem (A) quantifies energy_used; work quantification subsystem (B) quantifies ton_moved. Fusion layer normalizes units and time windows, enabling direct calculation of energy_per_ton. Telemetry interface exports fused results for logging and validation. SafetyAlert interface signals measurement anomalies (energy-work mismatch, sensor failure) for intervention. Verification requires: (1) sensor calibration validation, (2) fusion algorithm correctness testing, (3) end-to-end energy_per_ton calculation over agreed evaluation period, (4) anomaly detection sensitivity tuning.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single, unambiguous owner of DP3. Responsibilities are well-defined: (1) orchestrate energy accounting and work quantification subsystems, (2) normalize heterogeneous units and time windows, (3) fuse results and expose unified metrics, (4) detect and report anomalies. External dependencies are explicit: Vehicle (sensor drivers), Tasking (mission context), Telemetry (export), SafetyAlert (anomaly signaling), Network Link (remote calibration). No ambiguity or shared ownership. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform Tasking inputs (load profiles, expected work ranges) and SafetyAlert thresholds; they do not alter DP or IM allocation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize sensor data packet schema: Define canonical structure for fuel consumption, power draw, load cell, and displacement sensor outputs (units, sampling rate, timestamp, quality flags). Publish as contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution to prevent driver coupling.

Specify fusion latency SLA: Document maximum acceptable latency for fusion algorithm (e.g., <100 ms for telemetry export, <50 ms for safety alerts). Recommend asynchronous buffering and configurable fusion windows to decouple from Vehicle control loop timing.

Establish measurement normalization layer: Centralize unit conversion and time-window alignment logic in a dedicated normalization module within NGHE Solution. Include validation rules (e.g., energy-work ratio bounds, sensor health checks) to catch anomalies early.

Implement transactional calibration updates: Define atomic update protocol for sensor calibration and fusion thresholds via Network Link. Include version tracking, rollback capability, and consistency checks to prevent transient measurement errors.

Define time-window negotiation protocol: Specify how energy accounting and work quantification subsystems negotiate sampling rates and time windows. Recommend explicit buffering strategy and alignment tolerance to ensure fusion fidelity.

Add measurement quality metadata: Extend Telemetry interface to include quality flags (e.g., sensor health, calibration age, fusion confidence) alongside energy_per_ton results. Enable external consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to assess measurement reliability.

Document anomaly detection thresholds: Specify energy-work mismatch tolerance, sensor failure detection criteria, and SafetyAlert trigger conditions. Tie thresholds to worksite safety plan and operational policies via Tasking interface.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP3 decomposition is logically sound, the NGHE Solution module boundary is correct (0.92 containment, 0.08 diffusion), and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose six critical diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that are architectural in nature and require explicit sub-level decomposition to manage. These risks—sensor driver coupling, real-time latency creep, unit normalization brittleness, calibration update atomicity, time-window alignment, and measurement quality metadata—are not implementation details; they are design decisions that must be captured as sub-FRs and traced to sub-DPs and sub-IMs. Given high safety criticality, deferring these risks to implementation is unacceptable. Decomposing DP3 into sub-level FRs will make these architectural concerns explicit, traceable, and verifiable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Establish and maintain a standardized sensor data contract (schema, units, sampling rate, quality flags) between Vehicle and NGHE Solution to prevent driver coupling and enable robust fusion.
FR3.2: Quantify and enforce fusion algorithm latency bounds (telemetry export, safety alert) to prevent latency propagation into Vehicle control loops and external consumers.
FR3.3: Normalize heterogeneous sensor units (fuel, power, load, displacement) and align time windows across energy accounting and work quantification subsystems to ensure fusion fidelity.
FR3.4: Implement atomic, versioned calibration and threshold updates via Network Link with rollback capability to prevent transient measurement errors and inconsistent energy_per_ton calculations.
FR3.5: Negotiate and enforce time-window alignment between energy accounting and work quantification subsystems with explicit buffering and tolerance strategy.
FR3.6: Attach measurement quality metadata (sensor health, calibration age, fusion confidence) to energy_per_ton results and expose via Telemetry interface to enable external reliability assessment.
FR3.7: Define and enforce anomaly detection thresholds (energy-work mismatch, sensor failure, SafetyAlert triggers) tied to worksite safety plan and operational policies.

assumptions: Vehicle provides standardized sensor driver interfaces; if not, FR3.1 (sensor data contract) becomes a critical blocker.
Network Link supports transactional update semantics; if not, FR3.4 (atomic calibration updates) requires alternative protocol design.
Tasking interface provides mission context (load profiles, expected work ranges) to inform SafetyAlert thresholds; if not, FR3.7 (anomaly detection) cannot be properly calibrated.
Telemetry interface can accommodate quality metadata without breaking existing consumers; if not, FR3.6 requires backward-compatibility strategy.
Energy accounting and work quantification subsystems can operate asynchronously with configurable fusion windows; if not, FR3.5 (time-window alignment) requires synchronous redesign.
Sensor calibration updates are infrequent enough that transactional overhead is acceptable; if not, FR3.4 requires eventual-consistency alternative.
open_risks: Sensor driver coupling: Vehicle hardware evolution may break NGHE fusion logic if sensor data packet schema is not formally versioned and enforced. Mitigation: FR3.1 must define schema as a contract with explicit version negotiation.

Real-time latency creep: Synchronous fusion processing may introduce unacceptable latency into Vehicle control loops or telemetry export. Mitigation: FR3.2 must specify latency SLAs and enforce asynchronous buffering.

Unit normalization brittleness: Scattered normalization logic across sensor adapters and fusion code may become unmaintainable and error-prone. Mitigation: FR3.3 must centralize normalization in a dedicated module with validation rules.

Calibration update atomicity: Incremental or unversioned calibration updates may cause transient measurement errors and inconsistent energy_per_ton calculations. Mitigation: FR3.4 must implement transactional update protocol with rollback.

Time-window alignment: Misaligned sampling rates and time windows between energy accounting and work quantification may degrade fusion fidelity. Mitigation: FR3.5 must define explicit negotiation and buffering strategy.

Measurement quality opacity: External consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) cannot assess reliability of energy_per_ton results without quality metadata. Mitigation: FR3.6 must extend Telemetry interface with quality flags.

Anomaly detection miscalibration: Thresholds not tied to worksite safety plan and operational policies may trigger false positives or miss genuine anomalies. Mitigation: FR3.7 must establish explicit linkage to Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces.
evidence_needed: Formal sensor data packet schema (units, sampling rate, timestamp, quality flags) with version control and backward-compatibility rules.
Latency measurement and profiling data from Vehicle sensor drivers and NGHE fusion algorithm under representative load profiles.
Unit normalization rules and validation logic (energy-work ratio bounds, sensor health checks) with test cases covering edge cases and failure modes.
Transactional calibration update protocol specification with atomic guarantees, version tracking, and rollback procedures.
Time-window negotiation protocol and buffering strategy with alignment tolerance and fidelity impact analysis.
Measurement quality metadata schema and Telemetry interface extension with examples and consumer integration guidance.
Anomaly detection threshold specification tied to worksite safety plan, operational policies, and SafetyAlert trigger conditions with sensitivity analysis.
